page: Vultr voc-m-24c-192gb-4800s-amd is an instance in the Optimized Cloud Compute family. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor operating at 2.0 GHz. The configuration provides 24 physical cores and 24 vCPUs under a shared CPU allocation model, with a thread-to-core ratio of 1.0. Cache specifications include 32 KB of L1d cache, 0.5 MB of L2 cache, and 16 MB of L3 cache.

The server is equipped with 192.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Local storage consists of a bundled 4800 GB NVMe SSD. Network connectivity includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No graphics processing units are included in this configuration.

In benchmark testing, the server demonstrates strong performance, placing in the top 25% for both stress-ng single-core (2929.87) and multi-core (69533.1) CPU evaluations. However, the shared CPU allocation model introduces potential resource contention tradeoffs compared to dedicated CPU instances, which may affect consistency under sustained peak loads.

Given the 1:8 vCPU-to-RAM ratio and the substantial 4800 GB NVMe storage capacity, this instance offers high resource density. It is structured for memory-intensive and storage-heavy workloads such as database hosting, large-scale caching, and data processing pipelines where high memory capacity and local storage space are prioritized over dedicated CPU guarantees.

It features 24 physical cores and 24 vCPUs under a shared CPU allocation model, running at a clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 192.0 GB of RAM, providing 8.0 GB of memory per core, alongside a bundled 4800 GB NVMe SSD for local storage. In benchmark testing, the instance achieves strong results, ranking in the top 25% for both stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU performance. While the shared CPU allocation presents a potential tradeoff in processing consistency, the high memory-to-vCPU ratio and substantial NVMe storage capacity make this configuration highly efficient for memory-intensive and storage-heavy workloads. It is well-suited for database hosting, large-scale caching, and data processing tasks.
